attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine health check. She has no specific complaints. She has a medical history of hypertension and dyslipidaemia. Her medications include perindopril and rosuvastatin. She works as a lawyer, drinks alcohol occasionally, and does not smoke. Her parent developed type 2 diabetes at age 65.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 138/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her BMI is 28.5 kg/m².

Investigations reveal:

Fasting plasma glucose: 6.5 mmol/L (3.9-5.5)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Repeat fasting plasma glucose on another day
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a fasting plasma glucose (FPG) of 6.5 mmol/L, which falls within the impaired fasting glucose (IFG) range of 6.1-6.9 mmol/L according to the World Health Organization criteria. However, a single fasting plasma glucose in this range does not exclude diabetes, as some people with diabetes may have fasting glucose in this range but higher post-prandial blood glucose levels (BGLs). The patient has several risk factors for type 2 diabetes including age over 40, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, overweight status (BMI 28.5 kg/m²), and family history of diabetes affecting first-degree relative.

Perform oral glucose tolerance test is the most appropriate next step. This test can distinguish between impaired fasting glucose, impaired glucose tolerance, and diabetes, as some people with diabetes will only be identified by a high two-hour BGL value after a glucose load. This distinction is particularly important as the management approach differs based on the diagnosis.

Repeat fasting plasma glucose on another day is sometimes used to confirm a diagnosis, but in this case, guidelines recommend an oral glucose tolerance test to clarify the diagnosis, as fasting glucose alone may miss diabetes in certain people. Diabetes can be diagnosed when two separate FPG are ≥ 7.0 mmol/L or one single fasting BGL ≥ 7.0 mmol/L with symptoms. As the patient's FPG does not meet diagnostic criteria for diabetes, a repeat FPG ≥7.0 mmol/L in an asymptomatic patient would still not meet criteria for diagnosis.

Measure glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c) is an alternative diagnostic test for diabetes, but it is not the preferred next step when a patient has already been identified with IFG. The RACGP guidelines recommend OGTT as the follow-up test for IFG to assess post-load glucose metabolism, which HbA1c does not directly measure. While HbA1c might eventually be useful for monitoring, OGTT provides more comprehensive information at this diagnostic stage.

Pharmacological therapy such as starting metformin is not routinely recommended for patients with prediabetes (IFG or IGT) in Australia unless they have additional risk factors and have not responded to lifestyle interventions. The diagnosis of diabetes has not yet been established in this patient.

Begin lifestyle modification only is important for all people at risk, but should not replace appropriate diagnostic testing to confirm or exclude diabetes. The patient requires an OGTT to clarify her glycaemic status before determining the full management plan.
learningPoints: A fasting plasma glucose in the impaired fasting glucose range may still represent undiagnosed diabetes.
An oral glucose tolerance test is required to distinguish impaired fasting glucose from diabetes.
Lifestyle modification is the cornerstone of management for patients with impaired fasting glucose.
Patients with impaired fasting glucose have an increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease.
references: The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners. Management of type 2 diabetes: A handbook for general practice. East Melbourne, Vic: RACGP; 2020. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/7fe75f75-56e0-40e6-a433-12bd15537c5a/Management-of-type-2-diabetes-A-handbook-for-general-practice.aspx

attributes.name: Junior
attributes.description: Suitable for a junior, pre-clinical medical student
